speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Ruth Crane, this is tape 10. Ruth, when did your husband pass away?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband passed away on the 1st of August, 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: And to finish off just a couple of questions, what effect did your experiences have on the way that you've brought up your two children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it had an enormous impact on them. I don't-- don't think it's always for the positive. Because I think that i was the one that talked-- could talk about it. My husband couldn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when they started to come with questions, where are my grandparents, or why like this and why like that, where are your friends, you know, I had, again, at that stage, I felt that I had to tell them as gently and according to their ages.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I know some people are saying that they couldn't talk for the first 40 years. I don't know. I-- I don't know really which-- what was-- it was not a right or wrong. I just know that some people could, or they felt that they had to, because they owed this to tell. Because, if they would deny it, if they wouldn't say that-- like this is, to me, it was denying the lives of those people that have perished. And I felt that, if I survived, I am the mouthpiece. Those children, the million and a half of children, they cannot speak for themselves. They were innocent children, with their whole lives ahead of them. They could not develop their potential. And that's why I feel that every child deserves to have an environment that allows those children, regardless of religion and color or creed-- to allow those children to develop their potential. Because our children didn't have-- didn't-- were not given this chance because of hate and because of many other feelings that people felt. And instead of this fine energy that allows people to create wars, to create love and peace-- to put this energy into love and peace would be something which those children deserve. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's why I felt that, when my children asked me-- my children, no matter what, were alive. They were given a life. And they were given the choices and the chances that those other children didn't have. So basically, I can't say that I felt-- I felt sorry for my children. I felt a very deep-- I can't put that pain into words-- that they missed out. They don't know-- they-- they do not know what it is, really, an extended family. They don't know the love and the care of a grandparent.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is where a child-- this is where a child fills itself up with all of this love and care. And my children missed out. We had to be everything to those children. So I felt and, occasionally, I see in my daughter, in particularly, I see that she is sometimes-- that it's very-- to see-- When I tell her something, she doesn't realize that I'm doing it. She just feels my suffering. And this is-- she suffers with me. And it's-- it-- she always remembered what I told her. Whereas my son, he came. When he came, first time, he said, you didn't tell me enough. You did not, mom. And I told him as much as I could.

speaker: interviewee
text: So on the other hand, that I talked with my children and I answered them, I don't feel sorry for them because of this. Although I am sure that they would have been better off without it. But I know that both of them are very strong people. They're very loving people. So I really-- I don't know if I've got a clear cut answer for this. Ruth, you do a considerable amount of work for the Holocaust Center, here, in Melbourne.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I know that you do a lot of-- you give talks to schools.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: It just evolved like this. I attended a school of philosophy, for five years, that was affiliated with the London School of Economics and Sciences. And after a while, when I-- after a few years, when I progressed enough to really attend to other students, suddenly, I thought, you know-- suddenly, when I heard from people that were doing post-graduated-- post-graduate courses, and I heard from them, I don't know where I am going. I don't know who I am.

speaker: interviewee
text: How can I possibly teach children or influence children if I don't know who I am? I said, wait a second. I might not know exactly what's going to happen tomorrow, but I know who I am. So I realized, at that point, that I-- that I want to get involved more with the Jewish people. Because there were-- I had two or three friends there, Jewish ones. But that took five years. I went two or three times a week, so that was a large slice out of my time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I felt that, no, I'm-- I'm going to go get deeper into it. And I think I'd rather do something for my own community, for my immediate Jewish community. At that stage, I know it doesn't sound so-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not really what I wanted it to sound. But this is how I felt, then, that I am sort of-- being sort of-- going away from really something entering into the life, which I really owe to give back. I've learned certain things. And I was enormously grateful for it. But I decided, if I have to give back-- and I believe, if you achieve something, on whatever level it is, like my parents and grandparents before me, you've got to put a part of it into the community. But I think, more than ever, it's my husband who really was the inspiration. Because he always did it. When I was involved with the children and with other things, he was always involved, in the sort of way he dealt with people, on this kind of a level, where he assisted them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Ruth, just to finish off, a final question-- what message would you like to leave for anybody who would be watching this video in-- in the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just what I said before. At the end of the day, I think what's important is love and peace. And I think, till today, unless I feel this kind of welling up within me, where I can look at faces and when I can see the love there and a certain peace there, I can't see anything else. Nothing else matters. Only when I see the faces, when I see the people, when I see, when I perceive this, that it exist-- exists, and it does happen, only then, I see what's around me. And only then, I see-- I can smell the flowers. Otherwise, I can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- this is a very simple-- it's worthwhile. I know it might sound-- you know, in today's world, the world is very tough. It's very tough. And I know all of this. And I can be, if I-- you have to survive, obviously. If the going gets tough, the tough gets going.

speaker: interviewee
text: But without this-- those feelings, without the people-- the people is, no matter what you achieve intellectually or on any other level, it is people that matter. And without the love and care of people and being able to respond to this, I can't see what else life is about. And by the same token, I would like to say thanks to Steven Spielberg Foundation. I would like to-- to thanks-- to thank you, Reuben, for conducting this interview, with such sensitivity and care. I would like to thank Richard. And I would like to thank Lynn, who I think-- I feel we were sort of enclosed in those confines of this room for a short while. But I think, no matter how difficult it is, I think that whatever, it is a great privilege, because it reinforces my belief-- how wonderful. I happen to be one of those people that happen to meet all those wonderful people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I want to tell you that I feel privileged that I was able to do it, on-- in whatever way. And I wish you well. And I want to thank the foundation, Steven Spielberg Foundation, for conducting this. And best of luck.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think that you will see, probably, later on how much-- how meaningful. Because it has to have an extra meaning, as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Ruth.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're most welcome. [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Shh.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo of my great-grandparents, Gershon Winter and his wife, who passed away on-- of natural-- who passed away in natural causes before the war. And the rest are their children and grandchildren, who perished during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is your grandfather-- great-grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gershon, this-- you want me to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he in the front row?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he's in-- my great-grandfather is in the front row.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one in the front row?

speaker: interviewee
text: One on the right-hand side.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the left-hand-- left-hand side of him is his little granddaughter and his wife. Yes, you said that. This is a photo of my grandfather, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hershel Winter. He passed away before I was born. And this photo and the previous one, of my great-grandparents, I was lucky enough to receive after the war. As-- there was somebody that found this on the ground and picked it up and took it to Russia. And-- and I-- it was given to me about 10 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what year this photo was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo would have been taken, possibly-- I was carrying this photo with me when we ran. As a matter of fact, there's a story to it. I was-- I was so, sort of, full of-- my mother was telling me, always, about her father.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was-- all the way, I had this photo. And then, of course, it was lost. But I had this photo, with me, when we were running to Russia. And I-- I never left this out of my sight till later on when we came to Olkusz. So this would have been taken-- I'd say my mother was 14 years old when he passed away. So she was about 31 when the war broke out, so [LAUGHS] count. [LAUGHS] Early 19th century.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, yes. Yes. But I tell you exactly. Eight--

speaker: interviewee
text: Are we finished?

speaker: interviewee
text: --1922. On the left-hand side is my auntie, Ciocia Hania, Auntie Hania. She would have been, here, perhaps like 13 years of age, very young. And so Ciocia Hincia, the one in the middle, which she would to been-- I know it looks much older-- she would have been about 15 or 16. And on the right-hand side is one of their cousins or their girlfriend there. We were not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the two aunties, are they on your mother's side?

speaker: interviewee
text: The two aunties were on my-- my mother's sisters. And they were, when the war broke out, one was about 20. I mean, they were in their late teens or early 20s, something like this. And they both perished during the war. They both perished, I think, in the beginning of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo would have been taken [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the beginning of the '30s, probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you obtain this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, the same source that I received the others.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. It is a letter [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] written to my father's cousin in 1931. And the letterhead is my grandfather's, Icie Mayer Siegreich, the first and the second part. And then continues-- the letter, itself, is written by my grandmother, what I think, in a very beautiful Yiddish. And I think that it is a miracle that I have it. And I received it in 1985.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo taken in 1926. It was sent to me in Oderberg. Somehow, I received the letter and with four photos. And I, right there and then, I decided that I, no matter what, the two photos-- I never parted with those two photos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: These people is my father, Yulek, on the right-hand side, and my mother, Zosia, on the left-hand side. And this was before I was born, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good. What it says is-- this is written by my mother. And it states, we are offering this photo for our most respected parents, Zofia and Yulek.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is this written on, Ruth?

speaker: interviewee
text: What is this is written on?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the-- on the back of the photo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photo of my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: The one we just saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one you just saw, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good. When I look at this photo, I remember, vividly, when it was taken. It was-- my mother took me. My father demanded. He was in Czechoslovakia. And he demanded a photo of-- of his wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went with. I-- I mean, I didn't have a photo. I mean, my mother had the photo taken. But that was the first time when I tasted chocolate covered ice cream.

speaker: interviewee
text: She bought me a double chocolate. And I didn't know what it is. I thought it's chocolate. And I've bitten into it. And I bit into it. And there it was, this beautiful ice cream in the middle. And you know-- and-- and it compensated for it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this in the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this photo is my mother, Zosia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: End of 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get a hold of this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was sent, to me, to the first camp, Oderberg. And by some-- I was the only one that received those letters. And I was only one that had those photos.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had four photos, but the others, I couldn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- did you carry these photos with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I had them. When I was at work, I had them. We had this something that represented a mattress. And it was in the middle, somewhere there. I always, somewhere, where-- sort of where I could know, I could put my hand in and touch it. But somehow, they were looking, you know. And they were-- when we were not there, they were looking, sometimes, through some-- I don't know. Anyhow, the photos have survived. I have survived. And the photos have survived. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I don't know. This is a photo of my late husband and his siblings. The first one, on the right-hand side, is Bala, who perished with her family. Next is Motek. Then is Esther, who perished, really, during the last days in camp, just a few days before the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the last one is Gucia, who was together with me in Peterswaldau. And she survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo, I really don't know. This photo was taken-- this photo was taken before the youngest was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Bedzin, yes. But--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. --I really don't-- don't-- right now, I don't know the year. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't know the way they got it. Some-- after the war, you know? Or maybe my-- I don't know. Maybe she had a couple. I don't remember, right now, because I have it, now, for-- we have it, now, for many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is a photo of my two darling sisters. On the right-hand side is Lilka. On the left-hand side is Carmela. Carmela died in 1938. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, in 1939, Carmela died a few months before the war. And there was two years between them. So when this photo was taken, Carmela was four and Lilka was six.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Siemianowice. And the outline there, the shape in the back, is Mrs. Panczik.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who was our neighbor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get a hold of this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I went to-- to Siemianowice, after the war, she kept this photo. And she gave it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- This is a photo of my late husband, Motek, my darling husband, Motek, and his darling, youngest brother, Abramek. That was probably taken end of '38 or beginning '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bedzin. It was in the park, in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get a hold of this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just had it for many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just-- I think it came together with the other photo, of the four children, from the same source, again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good. This is a photo of my father-- late father-in-law, Moshe, on the left-hand side. On the right-hand side, his daughter, Estusia or Esther. He was-- at the time, he was 52. And she was 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1940, taken in 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bedzin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is that thing on his left arm?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's this arm band, white arm band, that all Jews had to wear. And there's a Star of David there. But of course, it's not visible in the photo.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you obtain this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've obtained this photo from my sister-in-law, who-- well, it was presented to her, a few months back, when she was in Israel. Last year, she went to visit Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one of somebody that survived from the family, and she had this photo. How does she have it? She doesn't know, really. They don't know exactly how they got it out. The cousin got the photo, because it's with the arm band. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo that I received, after the war, in Israel. And this was-- this was-- these are really passport photos. They were taken in 1943. And the idea was to use it as-- to enter Switzerland. And it didn't happen. But on the right-hand side is my grandfather, my paternal grandfather, Icie Mayer Siegreich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which row?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the top row, the top on the right-hand side--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. --Is my grandfather, Icie Mayer. In the middle is-- is-- right in the middle is Rysiek. Who's Rysiek?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] On the-- I don't-- his surname escapes me right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he a relative of yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was my cousin, my first cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left-hand side is Raizel Siegreich, who was my paternal grandmother. The lower three photos, on the left-hand side is Gucia, my auntie, my father's sister, Siegreich, and on the-- on-- in the middle is my aunt through marriage. I mean, she married my uncle, Abram, when I was already in camp. And he is on the lower right-hand side.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and you said these were taken in '43.

speaker: interviewee
text: These were taken, in 1943, in the midst of probably the worst possible time in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ghetto Bedzin. And and my parents are not there, because my-- my-- my parents didn't want to go, because I was in the camp. So they didn't want to leave me. So this is why they are not there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is a photo that was taken during the liquidation of Olkusz ghetto, during the three days. You can see the German guards in the front. And I am somewhere there, with my parents and little sister, somewhere there in one of those bundles, somewhere there towards the back. Because I don't recognize myself here. I can't see anywhere, here, in the front. So I am somewhere there. But I am definitely there in one of those bundles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1942-- 1942, towards the end of 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you obtain this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've obtained this photo-- I happened to see it in-- in one of the centers. And I couldn't believe it. I just-- it-- no matter what it depicts, for me, it represented, I was not born out of a stone, that I had parents and I had grandparents. I can't really describe it. But it-- it means a great deal. It's indescribable, the feeling that you really, actually, you know, that what you are saying, that they lived, they were there. You see, you see them. You see them standing there. Yes. And who knows what is in those bundles. I have no idea. Nobody knows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And this is also-- this is a photo that you've taken of the original photo, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't take it. But I asked who-- people who are in authority to take it. I ask-- I explained. And I asked if I may please have this photo, as I were there?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so they said, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a photo of a photo. And it depicts, in 1940, when a soldier, a German soldier, was shot on the outskirts of Olkusz. And Jewish people were blamed for it. And the Jewish men were rounded-- they-- well, they were rounded up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is where they are. And-- and they were, for quite a while, then, they were shot at random. And so a lot of them did not survive. My father came back, then. And there, you can see a German guard or soldier. And so it's just incredible. It's another miracle or something, you know? Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken, in Israel, in 1950. It's of myself and my husband, Motek. And it was taken a few months before we came to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo. This is a wedding photo of my darling son, Julius, with his darling wife, Ruth, taken in 1984.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Melbourne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a fairly recent photo taken in a park. I am at the top there. Next to me is my grandson, David. Sitting down is my daughter, Helen, my son-in-law, Colin, and on the right-hand side, little Emily. And they are wonderful children. They are a great inspiration and enrichment, providing this for me. And I think they're great.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is their surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schneider.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think it's a great blessing and privilege to-- to have them. This is myself and my darling husband, Motek.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This would have been taken about four years ago, so again 1992.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the wedding-- that was in Melbourne at one of the weddings that we attended.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- this is a recent photo of David and Emily. Looking at them, I-- I am filled with many mixed emotions. Although they are the biggest blessing then-- that any grandmother can be endowed with, but I can't help thinking about the children who perished. Listening to David, with his probing questions on the Holocaust, [SIGHS] I tried to avoid answering him. I thought that his parents should tell him. And then he said to me, Safta, you might as well tell me, because the teachers are telling us anyhow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was trying to-- to find-- to find the meaning, why is it so important for him to find out so much about, to come to the Holocaust Center? He was only nine years of age.

speaker: interviewee
text: But just recently, by the way, he's seeking in certain responses. What I see, what he's trying to find, it is he's trying to find in this, in his way, inspiration and enrichment in what he hears.

speaker: interviewer
text: It doesn't make sense that something like this can happen. And he's trying to make some sense from it, you know? So he's looking for-- for some ways where he can be inspired further. And when he comes here, the first thing he goes-- there is a book about-- on the 20th century. And he goes through the page where it is about the-- where-- where it says about Hitler and all of this, you know? And he comes back, triumphantly. And he says-- he says, each time-- what he does is he says, you see, he didn't-- you see, he perished. He was-- he was a monster. And what he got, he deserved. He didn't win. And the Jewish people won, you know? So he goes-- goes along. So somehow, it took me a long time to realize that this is what he's looking for.

speaker: interviewee
text: But [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] when I look at them, again, as well, I feel the love that I feel for all the other little children, for those that live now. And I truly hope and pray that they will be able-- and they will grow-- grow up, as this generation, of the young people, are the future politicians and decision-makers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I hope and pray that they will use their strength wisely. And when the future generations will live and grow, they can live and love in peace. Thank you.
